learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Intervals, Merge Intervals, Sorting, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine adding a new meeting to an already organized calendar. If the new meeting overlaps with existing meetings, they should be merged into one continuous meeting while preserving the schedule order.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine adding a new meeting to an already organized calendar. If the new meeting overlaps with existing meetings, they should be merged into one continuous meeting while preserving the schedule order.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Merge Intervals pattern whenever a new interval must be inserted into an already sorted list of non-overlapping intervals.
learning.signals: Whenever a new interval must be inserted into an already sorted, non-overlapping interval list, think of the Merge Intervals pattern.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are given an array of non-overlapping intervals sorted in ascending order by their start times and a new interval. Insert the new interval into the intervals such that the intervals remain sorted and non-overlapping after merging if necessary. Return the resulting intervals.

examples.explanation: The new interval overlaps with [1,3], so they merge into [1,5].
The new interval overlaps with [3,5], [6,7], and [8,10].

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Insert the new interval into the array, sort the intervals again, then perform a complete Merge Intervals algorithm.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Since the intervals are already sorted, process them from left to right. Add all intervals before the new interval, merge all overlapping intervals, then append the remaining intervals.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: Sorted intervals allow insertion without sorting again.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a new interval must be inserted into an already sorted, non-overlapping interval list, think of the Merge Intervals pattern.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: The intervals are already sorted and non-overlapping, allowing insertion and merging in a single linear traversal without sorting again.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a new interval must be inserted into an already sorted, non-overlapping interval list, think of the Merge Intervals pattern.
commonWrongPaths: Forgetting to merge multiple overlapping intervals.
Appending the new interval before merging.
Using '<' instead of '<=' when checking overlap.

commonMisconceptions: Forgetting to merge multiple overlapping intervals.
Appending the new interval before merging.
Using '<' instead of '<=' when checking overlap.

pattern.name: Merge Intervals
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a new interval must be inserted into an already sorted, non-overlapping interval list, think of the Merge Intervals pattern.

approaches.title: Insert Then Merge
approaches.idea: Insert the new interval into the array, sort the intervals again, then perform a complete Merge Intervals algorithm.

code.java: Insert interval, sort using Arrays.sort(), then apply Merge Intervals.

approaches.title: Single Pass Merge
approaches.idea: Since intervals are already sorted, process them in one traversal without sorting again.

approaches.title: Three-Phase Merge
approaches.idea: Traverse the sorted intervals once. First, add all intervals ending before the new interval begins. Next, merge every overlapping interval by expanding the new interval. Finally, append the remaining intervals.
approaches.intuition: The intervals are already sorted and non-overlapping, allowing insertion and merging in a single linear traversal without sorting again.
algorithm: Traverse intervals before overlap and add them.

code.python:
def insert(intervals,newInterval):
    ans=[]
    i=0
    n=len(intervals)

    while i<n and intervals[i][1]<newInterval[0]:
        ans.append(intervals[i])
        i+=1

    while i<n and intervals[i][0]<=newInterval[1]:
        newInterval[0]=min(newInterval[0],intervals[i][0])
        newInterval[1]=max(newInterval[1],intervals[i][1])
        i+=1

    ans.append(newInterval)

    while i<n:
        ans.append(intervals[i])
        i+=1

    return ans
code.cpp: Uses one-pass interval insertion and merging.
code.javascript: Uses single-pass merge of sorted intervals.

interviewFollowUps: How is this different from Merge Intervals?
What if multiple new intervals are inserted?
How would you handle interval deletion?
interviewTips: Discuss why only one pass is needed.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: Sorted intervals allow insertion without sorting again.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to merge multiple overlapping intervals.
Appending the new interval before merging.
Using '<' instead of '<=' when checking overlap.
